channel.title: Shit PH Said, Mostly By Anwar
channel.description: Recent content on Shit PH Said, Mostly By Anwar

item.title: Cabinet Maintains RON95 at RM1.99 — Anwar Calls It a 'Difficult Decision' While Dodging Subsidy Reform for the Third Year Running
item.description:
Said by: Anwar Ibrahim, Prime Minister and Finance Minister

Category: Economy

Date: 2026-03-11

📌 What Happened

Prime Minister Anwar Ibrahim announced on March 11, 2026 — following a Cabinet meeting — that the price of RON95 petrol under the Budi95 targeted subsidy mechanism will remain at RM1.99 per litre. He called it a “difficult decision,” noting that the market price of RON95 has risen roughly threefold due to the escalating US-Iran conflict disrupting oil markets and threatening Gulf shipping routes. RON97 had already risen to RM3.25, unsubsidised RON95 to RM2.67, and diesel to RM3.12. Anwar framed the decision as a sacrifice for the rakyat.

🔥 Why It Matters

This is not generosity — it is the collapse of reform. Anwar’s government promised targeted fuel subsidy restructuring as a cornerstone of fiscal discipline. The Budi95 mechanism, launched as a watered-down compromise, was supposed to be the first step toward removing blanket subsidies. Instead, a geopolitical crisis has given the government the perfect excuse to shelve reform indefinitely while spending billions on untargeted fuel subsidies ahead of widely expected snap elections. Every week the subsidy is maintained at current market spreads, Malaysia hemorrhages fiscal resources that could go to healthcare, education, or infrastructure.

💩 Why It’s Bullshit

Anwar has been “about to reform” fuel subsidies since he took office in November 2022. In 2023, it was “next year.” In 2024, it was “soon.” In 2025, the Budi95 mechanism finally launched — then immediately became a political tool rather than a fiscal one. Just five days ago, Anwar admitted he could only hold the RM1.99 price for “one or two months.” Now, with Hari Raya approaching and elections rumoured for mid-2026, the Cabinet “decides” to maintain it — as if there was ever a chance they’d raise fuel prices weeks before a festive season and a potential snap poll. This isn’t a difficult decision. It’s the only decision a government terrified of voters would make.

📜 Previous Cases

Malaysia has been trapped in the fuel subsidy cycle for decades. Najib’s government removed RON95 subsidies in 2014 with a managed float, only for Mahathir’s PH 1.0 government to reintroduce price ceilings. Anwar promised to break the pattern with targeted subsidies — means-tested, fiscally responsible, reform-driven. Instead, Budi95 kept the same RM1.99 price for the vast majority of motorists and excluded only foreign-registered vehicles and luxury cars. The IMF, World Bank, and Malaysia’s own fiscal policy advisors have repeatedly warned that blanket fuel subsidies are regressive, benefiting the wealthy more than the poor. Anwar knows this. He cited these studies himself when he was in opposition.

🎯 PH Callout

You told Malaysians you’d be different. You said you’d make the hard calls Najib and Muhyiddin wouldn’t. You quoted fiscal responsibility in every speech, promised targeted subsidies in every manifesto, and lectured the opposition about populism from every podium. Now you’re spending billions to keep fuel cheap during Ramadan, ahead of Hari Raya, ahead of elections you’re quietly preparing for. This isn’t reform. This is the oldest trick in Malaysian politics — and you swore you’d never play it. Every ringgit spent on untargeted subsidies is a ringgit stolen from the structural reforms you promised the rakyat.

Sources: The Star — Cabinet decides to maintain subsidised RON95 price at RM1.99 | Malay Mail | The Star — PM: Fuel subsidy can last up to two months (March 7) | paultan.org — Budi95 petrol price maintained (March 6)

item.title: Oil Past $100, Strait of Hormuz Shut, Rakyat Burning Fuel to Commute — Anwar Won't Mandate WFH, Freeze Tolls, Cap Food Prices, or Cut GLC Fat
item.description:
Said by: Anwar Ibrahim, Prime Minister and Finance Minister

Category: Economy

Date: 2026-03-11

📌 What Happened

The Iran-US war has pushed global oil past $100 per barrel, effectively shut down the Strait of Hormuz — through which nearly 80% of Asia’s oil transits — and sent Malaysia’s FBM KLCI plunging 2.55% in a single session on March 9. RON97 is up to RM3.25, diesel to RM3.12, and Anwar himself admitted on March 7 that the government can only hold RON95 at RM1.99 for ‘one or two more months.’ Freight costs are surging, food prices are next, and experts from NST to Hong Leong IB are warning of imported inflation hitting the rakyat’s kitchen table. Anwar’s response so far: a strongly worded tweet about solidarity, an emergency parliamentary motion condemning Israel, and Zafrul saying Malaysia is ‘prepared’ — without naming a single concrete domestic measure to protect workers and families from the economic fallout.

🔥 Why It Matters

This is not a theoretical crisis. Malaysians are already paying more at the pump for RON97 and diesel. Freight costs feed directly into food, medicine, and essential goods. The stock market is bleeding. And yet the Prime Minister — who is also the Finance Minister — has not announced a single emergency domestic economic measure. No crisis-mode intervention. No relief. Just speeches about geopolitics while the rakyat’s cost of living climbs in real time. Malaysia proved during COVID that the government can act decisively on domestic economic protection when it wants to. The difference is political will — and right now, Anwar’s will is directed at Parliament speeches about Zionist plots, not at shielding the people he governs.

💩 Why It’s Bullshit

Four things Anwar could do tomorrow with existing executive power — and won’t:

  1. Mandate work-from-home for all WFH-compatible jobs. Malaysia already has a Flexible Work Arrangement framework under the Employment Act. During COVID, the government mandated WFH and it worked. With oil past $100 and commuters burning RM300-500/month on fuel and tolls, a temporary WFH directive for office-based workers would immediately cut household fuel costs, reduce demand on subsidised petrol, and ease the fiscal pressure Anwar says he can only hold for two months. Instead, millions of KL workers sit in traffic burning fuel the government can barely afford to subsidise.

  2. Freeze or waive highway tolls during the crisis. The government already broke its toll abolition promise — paying RM591 million just to freeze hikes rather than remove tolls. A temporary toll waiver during a war-driven energy crisis is basic economic relief. Singapore waived ERP during COVID disruptions. Malaysia won’t even pause toll collection while fuel prices spike.

  3. Impose emergency price controls on essential food items. Freight costs from Hormuz disruptions are already feeding into import prices. Cooking oil, flour, rice, and protein prices will follow. The Price Control and Anti-Profiteering Act 2011 gives the government power to gazette maximum prices for essential goods. Anwar used this power selectively before. Now, when a global war is about to hit Malaysian dinner tables, silence.

  4. Freeze non-essential GLC and political appointment spending. The IDEAS report found 238 political appointments to FSBs and GLCs under Anwar. Every one of those comes with salaries, allowances, and perks funded by public money. During a war-driven fiscal crunch — with the subsidy bill ballooning and oil revenue uncertain — a responsible government would freeze all discretionary patronage spending and redirect it to rakyat relief. Instead, the appointments continue.

📜 Previous Cases

During COVID-19, the Muhyiddin government — for all its democratic illegitimacy — implemented a blanket WFH directive, moratoriums on loan repayments, cash handouts (BPN), and wage subsidies within weeks of the crisis hitting. The Ismail Sabri government continued with hybrid WFH arrangements and extended moratoriums. These were imperfect governments acting under crisis pressure. Anwar, who controls both the PM’s office and the Finance Ministry, has more concentrated fiscal power than either predecessor — and has used less of it in response to a war that is already hitting Malaysian wallets harder than the early weeks of COVID hit fuel prices. The precedent exists. The tools exist. The power exists. The will does not.

🎯 PH Callout

You told us you were different. You told us Madani meant the people come first. You told us having the PM also serve as Finance Minister would mean faster, more decisive economic management. So where is it? Oil is past $100. The Strait of Hormuz is disrupted. Your own deputy economy minister admits growth projections are shaky. Your own mouth admitted the RON95 subsidy has weeks left. And your crisis response is… a parliamentary motion about Iran and a tweet about solidarity? Mandate WFH. Freeze tolls. Cap food prices. Cut the GLC fat. You have the power. You have the precedent. You have the tools. Use them — or admit that ‘Ekonomi Madani’ was never about protecting the rakyat. It was about protecting the coalition.

Sources: Xinhua — KLCI plunges 2.55% | SAYS — Oil spike impact on Malaysia | The Star — RON95 subsidy 1-2 months | NST — Oil price surge could hit Malaysia | CNBC — Strait of Hormuz impact | Straits Times — Malaysia subsidy bill pressure | Malaysiakini — 238 political appointments | Asia Media Centre — Hormuz buffer

item.title: PH Backbenchers Call for Snap Election Dressed Up as 'Reform' — Hold GE16 With All State Polls, Save Money, They Say
item.description:
Said by: Azli Yusof (Amanah-Shah Alam) / Jimmy Puah (PKR-Tebrau)

Category: Politics

Date: 2026-03-11

📌 What Happened

On March 11, two Pakatan Harapan backbenchers — Shah Alam MP Azli Yusof (Amanah) and Tebrau MP Jimmy Puah (PKR) — called for all state assembly elections to be held concurrently with the 16th General Election. Speaking to FMT, both MPs argued that separate elections lead to lower voter turnout, waste public money, and produce state governments with weaker mandates. Azli said separate polls are “unfair to voters, especially those who live far away from where they vote.” Puah called it “simply too tiring for people to come out and vote twice.” Putrajaya spent RM725 million on GE15 in 2022 and a further RM420 million on six subsequent state elections in 2023.

🔥 Why It Matters

This is not a neutral policy suggestion. It is PH’s latest move in a coordinated signal — following DAP secretary-general Anthony Loke’s identical proposal last year and PH Johor chairman Aminolhuda Hassan’s recent push — to align the political calendar for a snap general election. By calling for simultaneous polls, PH backbenchers are doing the government’s messaging work: normalizing the idea of an early dissolution while packaging it as cost-saving reform. The real audience is not voters who are tired of going to polling stations — it is UMNO, GPS, and other coalition partners who need to be brought on board before the PM pulls the trigger.

💩 Why It’s Bullshit

PH did not care about “voter fatigue” when it suited them. In 2022, six PH-governed states chose to delay their state elections rather than hold them with GE15, citing monsoon concerns. Now, suddenly, the same coalition wants everything held together — but only because the timing benefits them. The opposition is fractured after the Bersatu-PAS split, the economy is holding, and Anwar’s approval is still intact. This is not reform. This is election engineering with a reformist paint job. If PH were serious about alignment, they would legislate fixed election dates — not send backbenchers to float trial balloons in FMT.

📜 Previous Cases

Malaysia has a long history of strategic election timing. Mahathir called snap elections when conditions favoured him. Najib dissolved Parliament early in 2013 and 2018. Even within the current cycle, the Sabah, Melaka, and Johor snap elections of 2020–2022 were all called for political advantage, not democratic principle. PH itself benefited from the chaos of misaligned polls — the August 2023 state elections became a de facto referendum on the unity government. The pattern is always the same: whoever controls the timing controls the narrative.

🎯 PH Callout

You promised institutional reform. You promised that governance would no longer be about political convenience. Yet here you are, using backbenchers to soften the ground for a snap election while pretending it is about saving the rakyat money. If you actually believe in synchronized elections, table a constitutional amendment to fix election dates — the way mature democracies do. But you will not, because the whole point is to keep the power to dissolve in the PM’s pocket. Stop dressing up electoral strategy as reform. Malaysians can tell the difference.

Sources: FMT — Hold GE16 with all state polls to ensure true representation, say MPs | The Vibes — Simultaneous polls could boost PH but test unity | NST — PH Johor proposes simultaneous elections

item.title: Police Question C4 Anti-Corruption CEO Under Section 124B — Anwar's Government Uses 'Plot to Topple' Probe to Interrogate Civil Society
item.description:
Said by: Bukit Aman (Royal Malaysia Police) / C4 Center (Pushpan Murugiah, CEO)

Category: Politics

Date: 2026-03-11

📌 What Happened

On March 10, 2026, the Center to Combat Corruption and Cronyism (C4) CEO Pushpan Murugiah was summoned to Bukit Aman and questioned for approximately 90 minutes under Section 124B of the Penal Code — activities detrimental to parliamentary democracy. The questioning was linked to the police investigation into an alleged plot to topple the government, reportedly because Pushpan’s name appeared in an email deemed relevant to the probe. But the interrogation went far beyond that email. Police questioned C4’s sources of funding, whether the organisation had received instructions to issue statements, and — remarkably — challenged C4’s right to comment on matters of national governance. C4 confirmed that several other political commentators and academics had also been summoned to Bukit Aman under the same investigation.

🔥 Why It Matters

This is not a routine police inquiry. This is the machinery of state — operating under a government that came to power on a reform mandate — being deployed to scrutinise, pressure, and delegitimise an independent anti-corruption watchdog. Section 124B is a serious charge. Using it to question a civil society leader about their organisation’s funding, autonomy, and standing to comment on public affairs is a textbook chilling effect. It signals to every NGO, academic, and political commentator in Malaysia: criticise the government’s handling of the MACC scandal, and you may find yourself in a police interview room explaining why you think you have the right to speak. This is the same playbook that Malaysians rejected when BN did it. The fact that PH is now running it should alarm every voter who believed the reformasi promise.

💩 Why It’s Bullshit

Anwar Ibrahim built his entire political career on the claim that he was persecuted by a government that weaponised institutions against critics. He spent years in prison. He rallied millions with the promise that under PH, the police would not be used to silence dissent. Now his government is hauling in the CEO of an anti-corruption NGO and asking — with a straight face — whether C4 has the standing to comment on corruption. The hypocrisy is breathtaking. C4 has been non-partisan since 2014. It criticised BN, it criticised PN, and now it criticises PH — and this is the administration that sends the police. The “plot to topple the government” narrative has become a catch-all justification for intimidating anyone who asks uncomfortable questions about Azam Baki, the MACC, and the corporate mafia allegations. If your anti-corruption credentials can’t survive scrutiny from an anti-corruption NGO, the problem is not the NGO.

📜 Previous Cases

This pattern is not new in Malaysian politics — it is simply new for PH. Under Najib, civil society leaders and journalists were routinely summoned under the Sedition Act, SOSMA, and the Communications and Multimedia Act for criticising 1MDB and government corruption. Under Muhyiddin, the Emergency Ordinance was used to suspend Parliament and silence dissent. Anwar himself was the most prominent victim of institutional weaponisation — twice jailed on charges widely seen as politically motivated. Now, under his own government, Section 124B is being stretched to cover the activities of NGOs whose only offence is demanding accountability from the MACC. Bersih was told to stop criticising MACC “during Ramadan.” DAP’s own youth wing warned of a “climate of fear.” And now C4’s CEO is sitting in Bukit Aman explaining why an anti-corruption organisation dares to talk about corruption.

🎯 PH Callout

You promised reformasi. You promised institutional independence. You promised that the police would never again be used as a political weapon. Now your police are questioning the CEO of an anti-corruption NGO about their right to speak — under the same “plot to topple” investigation that you’ve used to smear Daim’s family, attack Bloomberg, and deflect from the Azam Baki scandal. Every hour that Anwar’s government spends intimidating civil society instead of reforming the MACC is another hour that proves the Madani brand is hollow. If you want Malaysians to believe in your reform agenda, start by not behaving exactly like the governments you replaced.

Sources: FMT | BFM News

item.title: Sabah Rejected PH at the Ballot Box — Now Mustapha Sakmud Says He'll Be Their Voice in Putrajaya Anyway
item.description:
Said by: Mustapha Sakmud, Sabah PH Chairman / Sabah and Sarawak Affairs Minister

Category: Politics

Date: 2026-03-11

📌 What Happened

On March 11, 2026, Sabah PH chairman and Sabah and Sarawak Affairs Minister Mustapha Sakmud told FMT that Sabah PH will serve as a “conduit” to ensure state issues are voiced at the federal level. He said he intends to leverage his ministerial appointment — made on February 15 — to bring Sabahan concerns to Cabinet meetings and government agencies. “This is the role we aim to play, and we hope the people of Sabah will appreciate it when their fundamental needs are addressed,” he said.

🔥 Why It Matters

PH was annihilated in the November 29, 2025 Sabah state election. PKR scraped through with a single seat. DAP won nothing. GRS took 29 seats, Warisan took 25, and Sabahans made it abundantly clear they did not want PH representing them at the state level. Yet barely three months later, Anwar handed Mustapha a federal ministerial portfolio — Sabah and Sarawak Affairs — effectively using the federal Cabinet to insert PH into Sabah’s political space through the back door. This is not democratic representation. This is patronage as a substitute for a mandate.

💩 Why It’s Bullshit

You cannot lose an election this badly and then claim you are the people’s voice. PH won one seat in the entire Sabah state assembly. That is not a conduit — that is a rejection letter. Mustapha’s argument boils down to: “Sabahans didn’t vote for us, but we got a Cabinet post, so we’ll represent them anyway.” This is the exact kind of unearned political entitlement PH once accused BN of practising. The appointment rewards loyalty to Anwar, not service to Sabah. If Sabahans wanted PH to speak for them, they would have voted for PH. They didn’t.

📜 Previous Cases

This follows a long Malaysian tradition of using federal appointments to compensate for electoral losses at the state level. BN did it for decades in Sabah and Sarawak — parachuting federal figures into state politics through GLCs, agencies, and development corridors. PH, in opposition, railed against this exact practice. Now in government, they’ve adopted the same playbook. Anwar’s December 2025 Cabinet reshuffle — which created the Sabah and Sarawak Affairs portfolio — was widely seen as a damage-control exercise after PH’s Sabah wipeout. The pattern is clear: when voters reject you, buy your way back in through Putrajaya.

🎯 PH Callout

You promised reformasi. You promised that power would come from the people, not from Putrajaya. You told Sabahans for years that BN was using federal patronage to override their democratic choices. And now you’ve handed a Cabinet seat to a man whose coalition won one seat in an entire state election and told him to go represent the people who rejected him. If “the people of Sabah will appreciate it,” as Mustapha claims, then put it to a test — contest a by-election in Sabah on PH’s record. Otherwise, stop pretending a ministerial appointment is a mandate.

Sources: FMT — Sabah PH will bridge state needs with national policies, says Mustapha

item.title: 99-Year-Old Kampung Lee Kong Chian Residents Told to Get Out — RM50K Per Family, Hannah Yeoh 'Unable to Attend'
item.description:
Said by: Lo Su Fui, Deputy Minister (Federal Territories) / Hannah Yeoh, Minister (Federal Territories)

Category: Housing

Date: 2026-03-10

📌 What Happened

More than 150 residents of Kampung Lee Kong Chian in Setapak — Kuala Lumpur’s sole surviving private Chinese village, established in 1927 as quarters for Lee Rubber Group factory workers — held a peaceful rally on 8 March 2026 after 146 households received eviction notices in November 2025 ordering them to vacate by September. The land has been sold to a private company. Residents were offered RM50,000 per household in compensation, which they rejected, demanding People’s Housing Project (PPR) units instead. Federal Territories Minister Hannah Yeoh was absent from the village’s final Chap Goh Meh celebration on 10 March; Deputy Minister Lo Su Fui showed up, handed over RM10,000 for the dinner, and said he would “bring this matter back to the Federal Territories Department to explore how we can best provide assistance.” The government’s official position, per NST, is that it will “review the situation before deciding whether to intervene.”

🔥 Why It Matters

This is a textbook case of urban displacement dressed up as market transaction. Kampung Lee Kong Chian is not a squatter settlement — village chief Tan Lai Kee stressed the houses were built with formal approval from the original landowner. These are 230 households, many of them descendants of workers who built their lives around the Lee Rubber factory nearly a century ago. When the government allows a private sale to erase a community of over 1,000 people with six months’ notice and a RM50,000 cheque — roughly the price of a used Myvi — it tells you exactly where ordinary Malaysians sit in the hierarchy of development priorities. The Federal Territories Ministry, under PH’s Hannah Yeoh, is the body with direct jurisdiction over KL housing and land disputes. Yet the minister was absent, and the government’s posture is to “review” and “explore” — the bureaucratic language of stalling while the clock runs out on residents.

💩 Why It’s Bullshit

PH campaigned as the coalition that would protect communities from rapacious developers and crony capitalism. Anwar himself has spoken repeatedly about housing affordability, people-first governance, and the dignity of the rakyat. So when a 99-year-old village — with documented, legitimate housing built under formal approval — is handed a six-month eviction notice and a compensation offer that wouldn’t cover a deposit on a flat in Setapak, where is the reformist government? Hannah Yeoh sent a deputy with a RM10,000 dinner cheque. The deputy said he’d “bring this back” to the department. That’s not governance — that’s a holding pattern. If PH cannot protect a century-old KL community with clear historical legitimacy from being bulldozed by a private developer, then every “people first” slogan they’ve ever uttered is decoration.

📜 Previous Cases

This follows the same pattern as the Kampung Sungai Baru evictions in 2025, where residents were forced out amid redevelopment pressure with inadequate compensation and minimal government intervention. KL’s Kampung Baru — the last Malay enclave in the city centre — has faced decades of similar redevelopment threats, with successive governments oscillating between grand masterplans and inaction. The PH government has also pushed Nga Kor Ming’s Urban Renewal Bill, which would grant sweeping ministerial powers over property acquisition — a bill so controversial that even PH’s own backbenchers revolted. The pattern is consistent: communities with deep roots but no political leverage get treated as obstacles to development, while the government oscillates between performative sympathy and institutional passivity.

🎯 PH Callout

You promised Malaysia Madani — a civilised, compassionate Malaysia. A 99-year-old village with 230 families, descendants of workers who built this city, is being told to leave with RM50,000 and a handshake. Your Federal Territories Minister didn’t even show up to the last Chap Goh Meh these families will ever celebrate in their homes. You sent a deputy with dinner money. If this is what “reviewing the situation” looks like, then your review is a eulogy. These residents are not asking for handouts — they’re asking for replacement housing under PPR, a programme your own government administers. Either use the power you have to intervene, or stop pretending you govern for the people.

Sources: The Star — Setapak Village Marks ‘Farewell’ Chap Goh Meh | NST — Over 150 Residents Rally Against Gombak Eviction Plan | NST — Govt to Review Gombak Eviction Dispute | BFM News (Threads)

item.title: DAP Finally Demands RCI on MACC — Weeks Late, and PAS Correctly Calls It Political Theatre
item.description:
Said by: DAP Leadership / Ahmad Fadhli Shaari, PAS Information Chief

Category: Politics

Date: 2026-03-10

📌 What Happened

On March 10, DAP — the largest party in the Pakatan Harapan coalition with 40 parliamentary seats — formally called for a Royal Commission of Inquiry (RCI) to investigate the Malaysian Anti-Corruption Commission over allegations first reported by Bloomberg, including that MACC chief Azam Baki breached civil servant shareholding limits and that the agency was involved in corporate intimidation. PAS information chief Ahmad Fadhli Shaari responded by calling the demand “political theatre,” noting that DAP had remained silent when the same Bloomberg reports alleged that Prime Minister Anwar Ibrahim personally interfered in MACC investigations.

🔥 Why It Matters

This is the clearest example yet of DAP’s credibility gap on institutional reform. The party spent weeks after the Bloomberg exposé refusing to directly challenge Anwar’s handling of Azam Baki, even as eight PKR MPs led by Rafizi Ramli publicly demanded Azam’s suspension in mid-February. DAP’s belated RCI call comes only after its catastrophic Sabah election loss and growing voter fury — not from genuine conviction. When even PAS can accurately identify your reform posture as performative, something has gone deeply wrong with your party’s moral compass.

💩 Why It’s Bullshit

DAP had the numbers to force the issue from day one. Forty seats. The largest bloc in government. Instead, they sat on their hands for weeks while Bloomberg’s reporting exposed potential corruption at the highest levels of the anti-graft agency — and Anwar’s alleged interference in it. Now they want credit for demanding an RCI? That’s not accountability. That’s damage control dressed up as principle. And the most damning part: DAP still hasn’t addressed the Bloomberg allegation that their own Prime Minister interfered in MACC affairs. They’re demanding an investigation into everyone except the man they answer to.

📜 Previous Cases

This pattern is pure DAP under the unity government. After the Sabah election wipeout in November 2025, DAP pledged to “accelerate the reform agenda over the next six months.” Months later, the PM tenure limit bill failed partly because government MPs were absent, and DAP’s response was to blame the opposition rather than hold their own coalition accountable. On the Azam Baki shareholding scandal — which first surfaced in 2021 when DAP was in opposition and demanded action — DAP is now the party in government protecting the same man they once wanted removed. The pattern is clear: loud in opposition, silent in power.

🎯 PH Callout

DAP, you promised reformasi. You promised institutional accountability. You promised you were different. But when Bloomberg dropped the biggest anti-corruption story in Malaysian politics since 1MDB, your first instinct was to protect the Prime Minister, not the public. An RCI demand that comes weeks late and deliberately avoids the PM’s role isn’t reform — it’s theatre. If you want to prove PAS wrong, start by addressing the Bloomberg allegations about Anwar’s interference in MACC. Until then, Ahmad Fadhli is right: you are not qualified to champion this.

Sources: FMT | The Edge Malaysia | Reuters | US News / Reuters

item.title: Dzulkefly Promises — Again — to Crack Down on Workplace Bullying in Government Hospitals
item.description:
Said by: Dzulkefly Ahmad, Health Minister

Category: Health

Date: 2026-03-10

📌 What Happened

Health Minister Dzulkefly Ahmad announced on March 10, 2026 that the Health Ministry will “bolster enforcement” against workplace bullying in government health facilities. He said public hospitals should be “professional and ethical workplace environments” and pointed to a Public Services Department (PSD) circular that bans coarse language and verbal abuse in the workplace. He pledged to “enhance implementation” of the circular to ensure “no ambiguity” in handling misconduct — language that strongly implies the existing rules are not being enforced.

🔥 Why It Matters

This is not a new announcement. Dzulkefly has been making near-identical promises since at least August 2025, when the Health Ministry recorded 430 bullying complaints with only four under investigation. He launched “zero tolerance” pledges, announced new guidelines classifying verbal threats and mockery as bullying (due in October 2025), and even threatened to fire senior officials who conceal cases. Months later, he is back at the podium saying the same things — which tells you everything about whether the previous rounds of promises produced results. Housemen and junior doctors continue to report systemic harassment, and the ministry’s response remains performative announcements rather than structural accountability.

💩 Why It’s Bullshit

When a minister announces the same policy three times in under a year, the announcement is the policy — not the enforcement. Dzulkefly’s playbook is textbook PH governance: identify a real problem, hold a press conference, issue a circular, then do nothing until the next round of bad press forces another press conference. The circular he cited already exists. The guidelines were already launched. The zero-tolerance stance was already declared. If any of those measures had worked, he would not be standing up in March 2026 promising to “enhance implementation.” The problem is not ambiguity in the rules — it is impunity for senior medical staff who bully juniors, and a ministry that treats announcements as action.

📜 Previous Cases

In August 2025, the same minister made headlines for declaring zero tolerance on bullying after the case of “Dr S,” a houseman in Sabah allegedly bullied so severely she was confined to her room for years while the perpetrator moved abroad to pursue specialist training. In July 2025, the ministry announced new guidelines classifying insults, threats, and mockery as workplace bullying — guidelines supposedly launching in October 2025. Pressure group Hartal Doktor Kontrak has repeatedly called for systemic reform, not just guidelines, pointing to research showing high prevalence of bullying among Malaysian junior doctors across multiple government hospitals.

🎯 PH Callout

Anwar’s Madani government promised institutional reform and a government that works for the people. Dzulkefly is a senior PH minister in a critical portfolio, and the best he can offer after three years in power is re-announcing the same circular and calling it progress. Junior doctors — the backbone of Malaysia’s public health system — deserve more than recycled press releases. Either enforce the rules you already have, fire the people responsible, and publish the outcomes — or stop pretending the announcements mean anything.

Sources: Free Malaysia Today | The Malaysian Reserve (Aug 2025) | The Sun

item.title: Rafizi Quietly Drops NFCorp Bankruptcy Bid — The Anti-Corruption Crusader Makes Nice With the People He Once Exposed
item.description:
Said by: Rafizi Ramli, Pandan MP / Former Economy Minister

Category: Politics

Date: 2026-03-10

📌 What Happened

Rafizi Ramli, the Pandan MP and former Economy Minister who built his political career on exposing the National Feedlot Corporation (NFCorp) scandal, has quietly dropped his bankruptcy and winding-up proceedings against NFCorp director Datuk Seri Dr Mohamad Salleh Ismail and the company itself. A Notice of Discontinuance was filed on March 3, and the High Court struck out the case on March 10 without an order as to costs. Rafizi’s lawyer Razlan Hadri Zulkifli confirmed the matter was “amicably resolved.” The case stemmed from court-ordered costs owed to Rafizi after the Federal Court upheld his appeal in April 2022 — NFCorp and Salleh had paid RM300,000 in December 2022 but stopped paying the outstanding interest balance.

🔥 Why It Matters

Rafizi’s entire political brand was built on the NFCorp exposé. In 2012, he blew the lid off a RM250 million government soft loan meant for a national cattle-rearing project that instead went toward luxury condominiums and personal expenses — a scandal that brought down UMNO Wanita chief Shahrizat Abdul Jalil and became a symbol of BN-era corruption. He was charged under the Official Secrets Act for it. He went to jail for it. The courts vindicated him all the way to the Federal Court. And now, in March 2026, with his own MACC investigation hanging over his head over the RM1.11 billion ARM Holdings chip deal, Rafizi quietly makes nice with the very people he once dragged through the courts. The timing is impossible to ignore.

💩 Why It’s Bullshit

This is the man who told Malaysians he would never compromise on accountability. The man who said corruption must be pursued relentlessly, regardless of political cost. And now he’s walking away from money the courts said he was owed — not because he’s being magnanimous, but because the political calculus has changed. Rafizi is under MACC investigation. His former aide has fled to the UK. His political position within PKR is weakened after being dropped from Cabinet. When you’re no longer in a position of strength, you don’t pick fights — you settle. That’s not principle. That’s survival.

📜 Previous Cases

Rafizi has a pattern of selective aggression. He was relentless against BN-era scandals — NFCorp, 1MDB, Tabung Haji — when PH was in opposition. But once PH came to power, the fire dimmed. As Economy Minister, he championed the Central Database Hub (PADU) that drew criticism for data privacy concerns, and oversaw investment policies that favoured headline numbers over structural reform. His recent MACC trouble over the ARM Holdings chip deal, where he allegedly facilitated a RM1.11 billion investment in a company with questionable fundamentals, shows a man who has drifted far from his whistleblower roots. The NFCorp settlement is just the latest chapter in a long arc from crusader to career politician.

🎯 PH Callout

PH, you told Malaysians that Reformasi meant accountability without exception. Rafizi was your poster boy — the brave whistleblower who went to prison for exposing corruption. Now he’s settling with the people he exposed, dropping court-ordered debts, and making amicable deals while his own MACC probe gathers steam. If your best anti-corruption champion can’t even follow through on a case he already won, what exactly does PH stand for anymore? You don’t get to claim the moral high ground when your own people are cutting quiet deals in courtrooms.

Sources: Malay Mail | Free Malaysia Today | The Star | The Edge

item.title: Ramanan Claims 'Paid Agents' Are Behind Racial Tensions — Promises Cabinet Crackdown Instead of Addressing Root Causes
item.description:
Said by: R. Ramanan, Human Resources Minister / Aaron Ago Dagang, National Unity Minister

Category: Politics

Date: 2026-03-10

📌 What Happened

Human Resources Minister and PKR Vice-President Datuk Seri R. Ramanan held a special press conference in Putrajaya on March 9 flanked by PKR officials, claiming that “agents of provocation” are being paid to incite racial and religious tensions in Malaysia. He said the matter would be raised at Cabinet on March 11, and that arrests have already been made. National Unity Minister Aaron Ago Dagang echoed the call on March 10, urging police to “take stern action” against anyone disrupting harmony. Neither minister named the alleged provocateurs or their financiers — Ramanan specifically said he would not name them to avoid “making them famous.”

🔥 Why It Matters

This is the latest escalation in a pattern where PH ministers frame legitimate racial grievances and social media criticism as the work of paid saboteurs, rather than symptoms of real policy failures. Coming barely a week after Anwar’s “Zionist plot to destabilise the government” claims in Parliament, Ramanan’s press conference — complete with a PKR entourage — signals that the Unity Government is building a coordinated narrative to justify crackdowns on dissent. The vague language about “agents” and “provocateurs” without naming anyone creates a chilling effect: anyone who raises racial issues can now be framed as a paid agent. For a coalition that came to power promising civil liberties reform, this is a deeply authoritarian move.

💩 Why It’s Bullshit

Ramanan’s argument boils down to: the economy is doing well, the ringgit is strong, unemployment is low — so anyone complaining about racial issues must be a paid agent. This is textbook deflection. Racial tensions in Malaysia are not manufactured by shadowy provocateurs; they are the product of decades of structural inequality, uneven affirmative action, and a political class that has never seriously addressed inter-ethnic grievances. PH promised institutional reform. Instead, it is adopting the exact same “external enemies” narrative that BN and PN used for decades — blame outsiders, arrest critics, and call it national unity. Ramanan held this press conference flanked by PKR officials, making it a party event masquerading as a ministerial statement. The Human Resources Minister has no portfolio responsibility for racial harmony — that belongs to Aaron Ago Dagang. So why is Ramanan leading the charge? Because this is a PKR political operation, not a governance response.

📜 Previous Cases

This follows Anwar’s March 3 Parliament speech where he blamed a “prominent Zionist group” and Bloomberg for trying to destabilise his government — a claim Daim’s family has since sued him over, calling it an “outright lie.” Before that, PH used the Sedition Act and Communications and Multimedia Act against social media users who criticised the government — the same laws they once promised to repeal. The “paid agents” narrative is a recycled BN playbook: Najib’s government routinely blamed racial tensions on “DAP provocateurs” and “foreign-funded NGOs.” PH has now adopted the same framework, just with different villains. Every Malaysian government, from Mahathir 1.0 to Muhyiddin to Anwar, has discovered that blaming racial tensions on shadowy outsiders is easier than fixing the system.

🎯 PH Callout

You ran on a platform of protecting civil liberties, repealing oppressive laws, and building a “Malaysia Madani” where citizens could speak freely. Now your ministers are holding press conferences warning Malaysians to shut up or face prosecution, while refusing to name the people they accuse. If these “agents” are real, name them, charge them, and let the courts decide — you said it yourself, Ramanan. If you cannot name them, then stop using vague threats to chill public discourse. You are not protecting national unity. You are protecting yourselves from accountability.

Sources: Bernama | NST | The Star | The Vibes | Malay Mail | Bernama — Aaron Ago Dagang

item.title: Anwar Announces RM500 Cash Handout for Civil Servants and Mosque Staff — Hari Raya Election Season Begins
item.description:
Said by: Anwar Ibrahim, Prime Minister and Finance Minister

Category: Economy

Date: 2026-03-09

📌 WHAT HAPPENED: On 9 March 2026, Anwar Ibrahim announced Special Financial Assistance (Bantuan Kewangan Khas, BKK) 2026: RM500 for civil servants Grade 15 and below, RM250 for pensioners and veterans, and RM500 for approximately 70,000 mosque and religious personnel — imams, bilal, siak, marbut, KAFA and takmir teachers. Payments land on 13 March, days before Hari Raya Aidilfitri, when household spending peaks and political goodwill is most visible. Anwar holds both the PM and Finance Minister portfolios, giving him unilateral authority over the timing and targeting of such disbursements.

🔥 WHY IT MATTERS: This is textbook election-cycle spending: targeted, populist, and timed for maximum emotional impact during a religious holiday. Bundling civil servants and mosque personnel in the same announcement shores up two critical vote banks simultaneously — the bureaucracy and the religious establishment. The RM500 for mosque staff is a transparent attempt to undercut PAS’s monopoly on the religious vote, funded not by institutional reform but by direct cash transfers from public funds. When a PM who is also Finance Minister announces pre-Raya handouts with no independent fiscal check, the line between governance and campaigning disappears entirely.

💩 WHY IT’S BULLSHIT: Cash handouts before festive seasons is the BN playbook that PH spent decades criticising. Najib’s BR1M was mocked as “vote buying” by PH in opposition. Anwar’s BKK is the same thing with a different name. The inclusion of mosque personnel is particularly cynical: these roles are traditionally associated with PAS-aligned rural and semi-urban constituencies, making this a direct bid to compete with PAS on religious ground using public money. If you wanted to improve the lives of mosque workers, you’d reform their employment terms and pay scales permanently — not throw them a pre-Raya bonus when you need their votes.

📜 PREVIOUS CASES: Najib’s BR1M (later BSH) was the original festive cash handout programme, widely criticised by PH as populist vote-buying. Muhyiddin’s BPN during COVID was similarly timed for political impact. Every Malaysian PM discovers the electoral power of cash transfers before major holidays. Anwar promised to be different. He isn’t.

🎯 PH CALLOUT: You mocked BR1M for years. Called it bribery. Called it unsustainable. Now you’re doing the exact same thing, timed to the exact same calendar, targeting the exact same vote banks — with the added bonus of buying the mosque vote with taxpayer money. If this is what ‘Malaysia Madani’ looks like, it’s just 1Malaysia with better branding.

Sources: Malay Mail

item.title: Anwar Brings Forward RM15 Billion STR Cash Handout — Record Spending, Hari Raya Timing, Snap Election Vibes
item.description:
Said by: Anwar Ibrahim, Prime Minister and Finance Minister

Category: Economy

Date: 2026-03-09

📌 WHAT HAPPENED: On March 9, 2026, Prime Minister Anwar Ibrahim announced that Phase 2 of the Sumbangan Tunai Rahmah (STR) cash transfer programme would be brought forward to March 10 — weeks ahead of its originally scheduled date. The early disbursement will benefit 5.2 million recipients.

Anwar framed the move as helping Malaysians prepare for Hari Raya Aidilfitri. The total 2026 allocation for STR and SARA (Sumbangan Asas Rahmah) stands at RM15 billion — the highest in the history of the Federal Government’s direct cash assistance programmes.

This comes on the same day Anwar also announced a separate RM500 special financial aid for civil servants in Grade 15 and below (to be paid March 13) and mosque staff.

🔥 WHY IT MATTERS

Three things make this significant:

  1. Record spending on cash transfers. RM15 billion is not pocket change. This is the largest direct cash handout allocation any Malaysian government has ever made — and it comes from a PM who campaigned on fiscal discipline, subsidy rationalisation, and ending the culture of handouts.
  2. The timing is suspicious. Reuters reported on March 6 that government sources say Anwar could call snap elections as early as July 2026. Bringing forward cash payments right before Hari Raya — the single most politically sensitive festive period in Malaysia — is textbook pre-election cash politics.
  3. Structural reform is nowhere. Cash transfers are not economic policy. They are a painkiller. The RON95 fuel subsidy rationalisation has been delayed repeatedly. The promised targeted subsidy mechanism is still not operational. Instead of fixing the system, the government is writing bigger cheques.

💩 WHY IT’S BULLSHIT

Anwar spent years — decades — attacking BN’s BR1M cash handout programme as vote-buying, populist bribery, and fiscal irresponsibility. He called it a “crutch” that kept the rakyat dependent on government generosity instead of building real economic resilience.

Now he’s running the exact same playbook, except he’s spending more money and bringing payments forward to land right before Hari Raya. STR is BR1M with a rebrand. The only difference is the name on the cheque.

The “highest allocation in history” line is not a flex — it’s a confession. If the economy is performing as well as the government claims (4-4.5% GDP growth, record approved investments), why does the government need to spend a record RM15 billion on cash handouts? Either the growth isn’t reaching ordinary people, or this is about elections, not economics. Probably both.

Anwar urged recipients to “spend wisely.” Here’s a thought: maybe the government should spend wisely too.

📜 PREVIOUS CASES

  • BR1M under Najib (2012-2018): Anwar and PH relentlessly attacked Barisan Nasional’s Bantuan Rakyat 1Malaysia (BR1M) as unsustainable vote-buying. DAP called it “irresponsible fiscal policy.” PKR called it “bribing the poor.” Now they run a bigger version of the same programme.
  • 2024 Hari Raya handouts: Anwar brought forward STR payments before Hari Raya 2024 as well. This is now an annual pattern — accelerate cash disbursements to coincide with the festive season for maximum political goodwill.
  • RON95 subsidy delays (2024-2026): The government has repeatedly postponed targeted fuel subsidy reforms. Instead of fixing the subsidy structure, they keep layering cash transfers on top. The fiscal consolidation story — deficit down from 5.5% to 3.5% — looks less impressive when you’re simultaneously running record cash transfer programmes.
  • Snap election signals (March 2026): Reuters reported on March 6 that two government lawmakers confirmed Anwar could call snap polls as early as July. Cash before elections is the oldest trick in Malaysian politics.

🎯 PH CALLOUT

You promised “reformasi, bukan derma” — reform, not charity. You told Malaysians that PH would build an economy where people didn’t need handouts. You mocked Najib for BR1M. You called it a plaster on a broken bone.

Now you’re spending RM15 billion on the same plaster — and you’re proud of it. You brought the payment forward to land right before Raya, right before the rumoured snap election window. And you have the audacity to call this “Rahmah” — compassion.

This isn’t compassion. This is campaign spending filed under a different budget line. If you actually believed in reform, you’d be fixing wages, fixing subsidies, and fixing the structural reasons people need these handouts in the first place. Instead, you’re writing record cheques and hoping the rakyat forgets you promised something better.

Sources: Bernama | Malay Mail | The Star | FMT | The Edge | Reuters (snap election signals)

item.title: Anwar Condemns Iran's Attacks on Kuwait — Weeks After Parliament Motion Defending Iran Against US-Israel Strikes
item.description:
Said by: Anwar Ibrahim, Prime Minister

Category: Politics

Date: 2026-03-09

📌 What Happened

On March 9, 2026, Prime Minister Anwar Ibrahim called the Prime Minister of Kuwait, Sheikh Ahmad Abdullah Al-Ahmad Al-Sabah, to condemn Iranian attacks targeting Kuwait. He expressed condolences for the casualties and affirmed Malaysia’s support for Kuwait’s security and territorial integrity. This phone call came exactly one week after Anwar tabled an emergency motion in Parliament on March 2 condemning US-Israel strikes on Iran, during which he declared that Malaysia could ’no longer swallow’ Western human rights lectures and PH backbencher Syahredzan Johan called on MPs to boycott all US embassy events.

🔥 Why It Matters

The whiplash is the point. Seven days ago, Anwar positioned Malaysia as a defender of Iran against Western aggression, framing the conflict in anti-imperialist terms and using it to score domestic political points with his base. Now Iran is the aggressor striking a Gulf neighbour, and Anwar has to condemn the same country he was defending. This isn’t principled foreign policy — it’s reactive, opportunistic positioning that changes with the news cycle. Malaysia’s stance on the Middle East conflict now reads less like a coherent doctrine and more like whichever statement plays best on the day it is made.

💩 Why It’s Bullshit

You cannot table an emergency motion defending Iran on Monday and condemn Iran the following Monday and pretend both positions are part of a coherent foreign policy framework. Either Malaysia has a principled stance on sovereignty and the use of force — in which case it should have been more measured on March 2 instead of turning Parliament into a political rally — or it’s making it up as it goes. The Syahredzan boycott call looks especially absurd now: should MPs also boycott Iranian embassy events? The March 2 motion was not about principle. It was about performance. And this week’s condemnation proves it.

📜 Previous Cases

Malaysia’s foreign policy under Anwar has been marked by reactive, headline-driven positioning. The government has oscillated between courting Western investment and denouncing Western hypocrisy, between engaging China on trade and pushing back on South China Sea issues, depending on the domestic audience. Mahathir did the same thing — his famous ‘Jews rule the world’ speech at OIC in 2003 was followed by continued quiet engagement with Western financial institutions. The pattern is Malaysian — grand rhetorical gestures for the gallery, pragmatic retreats behind closed doors.

🎯 PH Callout

PH, if you want Malaysia to be taken seriously on the world stage, pick a lane. You turned Parliament into a solidarity rally for Iran seven days ago. Your own backbencher called for a boycott of US embassy events. Now you’re calling Kuwait to condemn the same country you were defending. What is Malaysia’s actual position? Is it anti-aggression regardless of who the aggressor is? Then say that consistently. Is it pro-Muslim solidarity? Then explain why you’re condemning a Muslim-majority country attacking another Muslim-majority country. The rakyat deserves a foreign policy, not a mood board.

Sources: QNA | Straits Times

item.title: Anwar Launches Capital Market Masterplan, Says 'Reform Non-Negotiable' — While Every Actual Reform Gets Delayed or Killed
item.description:
Said by: Anwar Ibrahim, Prime Minister

Category: Economy

Date: 2026-03-09

📌 WHAT HAPPENED: Prime Minister Datuk Seri Anwar Ibrahim delivered the keynote address at the launch of the Securities Commission’s Capital Market Masterplan 2026–2030 (CMP4) on March 9, 2026. He declared that “the government has made reform non-negotiable” and that Malaysia’s capital market “must drive shared prosperity, not just profit.”

The CMP4 targets growing Malaysia’s capital market to RM6.3 trillion by 2030 and includes initiatives like the “MY Value Up” program to boost PLC valuations.

Also present were Second Finance Minister Datuk Seri Amir Hamzah Azizan and SC Chairman Datuk Mohammad Faiz Azmi.

🔥 WHY IT MATTERS

The phrase “reform non-negotiable” would be powerful if it came from a government that was actually delivering reform. Instead, it comes from the same Prime Minister whose government, in the past two weeks alone:

  • Failed to pass the PM tenure limit bill because 8 of its own MPs didn’t show up (March 3)
  • Postponed the AG-Public Prosecutor separation bill for a 3-month committee review — a core reform promise delayed yet again (March 4)
  • Refused to suspend MACC chief Azam Baki despite mounting allegations, opting instead to let his contract quietly expire in May (reported March 9)
  • Delayed RON95 reform repeatedly — Anwar himself admitted he could only hold the price “one or two more months” (March 7) “Reform non-negotiable” is a slogan delivered at a securities industry event while actual institutional reform — the kind that strengthens governance and accountability — keeps getting pushed back, watered down, or abandoned.

💩 WHY IT’S BULLSHIT

Anwar uses “reform” the way a luxury brand uses “exclusive” — it is a marketing word detached from the product.

The Capital Market Masterplan is a Securities Commission document. It is a technocratic roadmap for market growth, IPO pipelines, and PLC valuations. That is fine. But Anwar does not get to stand at a podium, declare “reform non-negotiable,” and receive credit for reform when:

  • The Attorney General still doubles as the Public Prosecutor — a conflict of interest PH has promised to fix since 2018.
  • The MACC chief remains in office despite allegations that would have triggered immediate suspension in any serious anti-corruption jurisdiction.
  • Institutional reforms that require political courage — not just SC planning documents — are systematically delayed. Capital market growth is not reform. Raising PLC valuations is not reform. “Shared prosperity” is not reform. Reform is when you change the rules that protect the powerful. Anwar has not done that.

📜 PREVIOUS CASES

  • 2018–2020 (PH 1.0): The first PH government also promised AG-PP separation, IPCMC, and institutional reform. None were completed before the Sheraton Move collapsed the government.
  • March 4, 2026: Fahmi Fadzil announced the AG-PP separation bill would be delayed for a 3-month parliamentary committee review — the latest in a long line of postponements.
  • March 3, 2026: The PM tenure limit constitutional amendment failed because PH couldn’t muster its own full attendance — 8 government and independent MPs were absent.
  • December 2025: Anwar halfed Najib’s sentence while the 1MDB debt — RM50 billion — remained on the rakyat’s books.
  • The pattern is consistent: PH announces bold reform frameworks at formal events, then delays or abandons the actual legislative and institutional changes required to implement them.

🎯 PH CALLOUT

You say reform is “non-negotiable.” Then why does every reform end up being negotiated away?

The AG-PP separation — delayed. The PM tenure limit — failed on your own attendance. Azam Baki — still in office. RON95 — still subsidised with no timeline. IPCMC — abandoned.

You have the parliamentary majority. You have the institutional mandate. You have the public goodwill — or what remains of it.

What you don’t have is the political will to spend your coalition capital on anything that doesn’t benefit the coalition itself.

Stop telling the Securities Commission audience that reform is non-negotiable. Start telling your own Cabinet.

Sources: The Star | NST | The Star (CMP4 keynote) | The Edge | Bernama

item.title: Anwar Repeats 'Aid Based on Need, Not Race' in Dewan Negara — Same Script, Different Session, Zero Structural Change
item.description:
Said by: Anwar Ibrahim, Prime Minister

Category: Politics

Date: 2026-03-09

📌 WHAT HAPPENED

Prime Minister Datuk Seri Anwar Ibrahim told the Dewan Negara on March 9, 2026, that government aid and assistance are allocated based on need, not race. He affirmed that the Madani government would “remain fair to all states” and that assistance targets those most in need regardless of background.

This is not the first time Anwar has used this exact line. In August 2025, he told the same Dewan Negara the same thing — almost word for word — as reported by The Star and NST. Before that, the 13th Malaysia Plan (13MP) was framed around the same “needs-based, not race-based” rhetoric.

🔥 WHY IT MATTERS

This is one of PH’s most repeated slogans. It was central to the Madani Economy pitch, and it resonates with voters who are tired of race-based distribution systems that benefit political insiders rather than the genuinely poor.

But slogans are not policy. The question is simple: has the government actually restructured any major Bumiputera-specific allocation program since taking power? Has any race-based quota been reformed? Has any procurement preference been revised to be purely needs-based?

The answer, by all available evidence, is no. The 13MP talked about shifting to needs-based frameworks, but core Bumiputera economic programs, GLICs, and procurement carve-outs remain structurally unchanged. The Poverty Line Income (PGK) thresholds have been updated — a technocratic adjustment — but the architecture of race-based distribution is untouched.

💩 WHY IT’S BULLSHIT

Saying “aid is based on need, not race” while leaving every race-based allocation structure intact is not reform. It is branding.

Anwar is not lying — technically, some aid programs like SARA and certain welfare payments do target low-income households regardless of race. But the big-ticket items — Bumiputera equity targets, MARA allocations, Bumiputera-only procurement set-asides, GLC preferential hiring — remain untouched. Nobody in the Madani government has proposed reforming any of these.

What Anwar is doing is classic misdirection: point to the small welfare programs that are needs-based, repeat the slogan, and hope nobody asks about the structural architecture that makes Malaysia’s economy one of the most racially stratified in the region.

Repeat it enough times in enough Dewan Negara sessions and maybe people will believe it. That is the strategy.

📜 PREVIOUS CASES

  • August 2025: Anwar told Dewan Negara that “allocations are based on needs, not race.” Same chamber, same script. (The Star, 25 Aug 2025)
  • July 2025: The 13MP was launched with the framing of shifting from race-based to needs-based policies. (Malay Mail, 31 Jul 2025)
  • Deputy Economy Minister Hanifah Hajar Taib (Dec 2025): Told Parliament the government was “reviewing” the use of disposable income as a fairer benchmark for aid eligibility — meaning even the basic measurement tools hadn’t been reformed yet, over two years into the administration. (The Star, 4 Dec 2025)
  • The core contradiction has existed since Anwar took office: the Madani government promises needs-based reform while relying on UMNO — the party whose entire political model depends on race-based distribution — as its coalition anchor.

🎯 PH CALLOUT

You ran on “Reformasi.” You promised a Malaysia where race would not determine access to opportunity. You told the rakyat that Madani meant fairness.

Three years later, every single race-based structural program remains intact. Not one has been reformed. Not one has been publicly reviewed. Not one has been opened to needs-based criteria.

And yet you stand in Dewan Negara — for the second time in less than a year — and say the same line: “based on need, not race.”

If that were true, show us the policy paper. Show us the program that was restructured. Show us the quota that was reformed.

Until then, this is not a policy. It is a press release.

Sources: Bernama via Facebook | The Star (Aug 2025) | NST (Aug 2025) | Malay Mail (13MP, Jul 2025) | The Star (Dec 2025)

item.title: Anwar Won't Suspend Azam Baki — Will Just Let His Contract Expire in May While the Country Burns
item.description:
Said by: Anwar Ibrahim, Prime Minister / Straits Times sources

Category: Politics

Date: 2026-03-09

📌 WHAT HAPPENED: The Straits Times reported on March 9, 2026 — citing high-level government and legal sources — that Prime Minister Anwar Ibrahim has decided not to renew MACC chief commissioner Tan Sri Azam Baki’s tenure when it expires on May 12, 2026. This would mark the end of Azam’s fourth annual extension since reaching retirement age in 2023. According to the report, Anwar has “repeatedly stressed to Cabinet in recent weeks that Azam won’t be renewed.” But crucially, Anwar is not suspending Azam now. He is not ordering an independent public inquiry. He is not referring the matter to Parliament. He is simply going to let the clock run out — two more months of an anti-corruption chief accused of being the head of a “corporate mafia.” This comes after Bloomberg reported that the MACC under Azam had abused its powers to extort and manipulate corporate figures; Azam was found to have breached civil servant shareholding rules multiple times, most recently in January 2026, holding 17.7 million shares in Velocity Capital Bhd; former Economy Minister Rafizi Ramli and 8 PKR MPs publicly demanded Azam’s immediate suspension; Bersih demanded resignation and organised protests; DAP Secretary-General Anthony Loke called the government’s response “inadequate”; and a Feb 15 rally in Kuala Lumpur demanded Azam’s arrest.

🔥 WHY IT MATTERS: This is not a win. This is damage control dressed up as a decision. Anwar is letting Azam Baki serve out his remaining two months while the MACC remains under a cloud of allegations that it operates as an extortion racket. Every investigation the MACC conducts between now and May 12 is tainted. Every raid, every summons, every prosecution carries the stench of an agency whose credibility has collapsed. The Straits Times quoted advisory firm Viewfinder Global Affairs: “That is a win that DAP will have to take and try to convince their supporters that they are making progress. It won’t be easy as the damage has been done from over three years of stalled reforms.” Risk consultancy KRA Group’s Amir Fareed Rahim was blunter: “Getting to the bottom of issues surrounding the MACC is a baseline requirement before talking about other reforms.” DAP has brought forward its national congress from September to July to decide whether to stay in the Anwar administration — based on whether reform progress accelerates in the first half of 2026. Letting Azam ride out his contract is not acceleration. It is coasting.

💩 WHY IT’S BULLSHIT: Let’s count the ways this is a failure. 1. Anwar renewed Azam three times. Every year since 2023, Anwar personally extended the tenure of a man his own coalition had demanded be suspended back in 2021-2022. He had the power to end this years ago. He chose not to. 2. PH campaigned on parliamentary vetting of the MACC chief. In GE15, Pakatan Harapan promised that the appointment of the MACC chief would be subject to parliamentary scrutiny. It is now 2026. That never happened. Azam was renewed by executive fiat, three times, with zero parliamentary oversight. 3. “Not renewing” is not accountability. Suspension triggers an investigation. Non-renewal just means Azam walks away in May with his pension, his dignity intact, and zero legal consequences for the alleged abuses under his watch. The difference matters. 4. Anwar called the MACC allegations a Zionist conspiracy. Instead of confronting the institutional rot, Anwar told Parliament on March 3 that the criticism of the MACC was part of a “Zionist plot to topple the government.” Police are now investigating critics under Section 124B — a law that carries a 20-year jail term. This is not reform. This is Najib-era playbook. 5. Two more months of a compromised agency. The MACC is currently investigating the Daim family for an alleged “plot to topple the government.” The MACC is investigating critics of Azam Baki under sedition-adjacent laws. And the head of this agency is a man accused of running a corporate shakedown operation. Letting him serve two more months is not prudent. It is reckless.

📜 PREVIOUS CASES: 2021 — First shareholding scandal: Azam Baki was found to own over a million shares in a listed company, breaching civil service rules. NGOs and PH parties — including PKR and DAP — demanded action from then-PM Ismail Sabri. Nothing happened. 2022 — PH election promise: PH’s GE15 manifesto included a commitment to have Parliament vet the MACC chief’s appointment. After winning, Anwar quietly shelved the idea and renewed Azam by executive decision — three years running. 2023-2025 — Three renewals: Anwar extended Azam’s tenure annually despite the unresolved 2021 scandal, growing civil society pressure, and reports of MACC overreach. February 2026 — Bloomberg exposé: Bloomberg reported systematic abuses by the MACC, including alleged collusion between MACC top brass and business figures. This triggered street protests, the “Arrest Azam Baki” rally, and demands from within PH for immediate suspension. November 2025 — Sabah wipeout: PH won just 1 of 22 seats in the Sabah state election. DAP lost every seat it contested. Among the issues: perceived selective prosecution linked to MACC investigations.

🎯 PH CALLOUT: You promised parliamentary oversight of the MACC chief. You never delivered. You renewed Azam Baki three times. You ignored the 2021 scandal. You ignored the Bloomberg revelations for weeks. When your own MPs demanded action, you called the criticism a Zionist conspiracy. And now your big move is to not renew his contract — the bare minimum — while letting him serve two more months at the helm of an agency accused of operating like a protection racket. This is not reform. This is running out the clock and calling it progress. Anwar Ibrahim once said: “We will not tolerate corruption in any form.” He has now tolerated it in the most visible form imaginable — at the very top of the agency meant to fight it — for over three years. If you want Malaysians to believe you are different from your predecessors, act like it. Suspend Azam Baki now. Establish a royal commission of inquiry. Refer the MACC chief appointment to Parliament, as you promised. Stop hiding behind task forces and conspiracy theories. The rakyat are watching. And they are not impressed.

Sources: The Straits Times | The Edge Malaysia — Rafizi calls for suspension | SCMP — Malaysia considers royal commission | Malaysiakini via Facebook — 8 PKR MPs demand suspension | Reuters — Anwar tells parliament of plot | CNA — Anwar’s perfect storm

item.title: Anwar's Government Fights 531,976 Retirees in Court — Wins Appeal to Block RM1.7 Billion Pension Payout
item.description:
Said by: Federal Government / Senior Federal Counsel Sallehuddin Ali

Category: Economy

Date: 2026-03-09

📌 What Happened

On 9 March 2026, the Court of Appeal unanimously overturned a High Court order that would have required Anwar Ibrahim’s government to pay RM1.7 billion in pension arrears to 531,976 former civil servants. The three-judge panel — led by Justice Azhahari Kamal Ramli, with Justices Lim Chong Fong and Meor Hashimi Abdul Hamid — ruled that the pensioners’ application was res judicata (already adjudicated) and constituted an “abuse of court process.” The government, represented by Senior Federal Counsel Sallehuddin Ali, successfully argued that the 2016 Public Service Department Circular (PP 1/2016) was an “enhancement” to the civil service scheme, not a mandatory salary revision triggering pension adjustments. The original case was brought by Aminah Ahmad, a retired Ministry of Foreign Affairs officer, on behalf of 56 other retirees in 2017.

🔥 Why It Matters

This is the Madani government actively going to court to avoid paying half a million retirees money they were legally awarded. The average payout per retiree would have been roughly RM3,195 — not a fortune, but real money for people on fixed incomes. The government chose to fight this rather than honour a High Court order. This is a government that spends billions on Hari Raya cash handouts and election-year STR payments, but when pensioners win a court ruling for pension adjustments based on the government’s own 2016 circular, Putrajaya deploys federal counsel to crush the claim on a technicality. The signal is clear: if you’re politically useful, you get cash. If you’re a retiree with a legal claim, you get lawyers.

💩 Why It’s Bullshit

Anwar Ibrahim built his career on defending the little guy. His government now argues that a PSD circular meant to adjust pensions in line with civil service salary changes was merely an “enhancement” — bureaucratic doublespeak for “we wrote it, but we didn’t mean it.” The Court of Appeal’s ruling that this was “abuse of process” doesn’t mean the pensioners’ grievance is illegitimate — it means the government found a procedural exit ramp and took it. Meanwhile, Putrajaya approved RM15 billion in STR cash handouts just days earlier. The message: Anwar’s government has money for voters, not for retirees who already served the country.

📜 Previous Cases

Malaysian governments have a long history of treating pension adjustments as political favours rather than legal obligations. The 2013 amendment to the Pension Adjustment Act — which provided retirees with a fixed annual 2% increase — was itself invalidated by the Court of Appeal in 2022, stripping retirees of a guaranteed mechanism. Every administration since has treated pension reform as a hot potato: promise it during campaigns, dodge it in office. The Madani government is no different. Anwar’s own 2022 election manifesto spoke of “dignifying” civil servants and retirees, but the legal strategy his government is pursuing tells a different story.

🎯 PH Callout

You promised kerajaan Madani — a civilised government that upholds justice and human dignity. Half a million people who spent their careers serving this country were told by a High Court that they are owed pension adjustments. Your government’s response was not to pay, not to negotiate, but to appeal and win on a technicality. You found RM15 billion for pre-Raya handouts but fought tooth and nail against RM1.7 billion for pensioners. That is not reform. That is not Madani. That is the same old Putrajaya arithmetic: spend where the votes are, litigate where they aren’t.

Sources: NST | Malay Mail | The Star | Bernama | SAYS | The Edge

item.title: Daim's Family Sues Anwar's Government Over 'Zionist Plot' Claims — Calls Police Probe Unlawful and Unconstitutional
item.description:
Said by: Anwar Ibrahim, Prime Minister / Daim Zainuddin’s Family (Respondents)

Category: Politics

Date: 2026-03-09

📌 What Happened

The family of late former finance minister Daim Zainuddin — his widow Naimah Abdul Khalid, two sons, and two family lawyers — filed a lawsuit at the Kuala Lumpur High Court on March 7, 2026, seeking a declaration that the police investigation into the alleged plot to topple Anwar’s government is unlawful, unconstitutional, and tainted by bad faith. This comes after Anwar told Parliament on March 3 that a suspect in the alleged conspiracy had engaged an international PR firm to launch coordinated attacks on national institutions, claiming the plot involved ‘a prominent Zionist group,’ foreign lobbyists, bankers, and international media agencies.

🔥 Why It Matters

This is no longer just a political narrative — it is now a legal confrontation. Anwar built his case for the ‘Zionist plot’ on parliamentary privilege and police reports, but a court challenge forces his government to produce actual evidence or face the embarrassment of having its probe declared unconstitutional. Critics are already pointing out the hypocrisy: Anwar’s own political camp sought foreign support, engaged international media, and lobbied overseas governments extensively during his decades in opposition. The lawsuit turns Anwar’s own reformist framing against him — if this government truly believes in due process and rule of law, it cannot simultaneously brand overseas lobbying as subversion when it’s done by opponents.

💩 Why It’s Bullshit

Anwar spent years as an opposition leader cultivating ties with international media, foreign governments, and global civil society to pressure Malaysian governments. His allies flew to Washington, London, and Geneva to make their case. That was advocacy. But when Daim’s family allegedly does something similar, it’s a ‘Zionist conspiracy to topple the government.’ The double standard is not subtle — it is the entire structure. This is a prime minister who promised accountability and institutional reform, now using the language of conspiracy theories and securitisation to deflect from legitimate questions about MACC misconduct and elite impunity. The ‘Zionist plot’ framing is a political shield, not a legal argument.

📜 Previous Cases

Malaysian governments have a long tradition of invoking external threats to shut down domestic dissent. Mahathir blamed ‘Jewish speculators’ for the 1997 financial crisis. Najib’s government accused foreign interference when the 1MDB scandal broke. Anwar himself was the target of politically motivated prosecutions dressed up in legal language. The playbook is familiar: when the pressure mounts, find a foreign enemy. Anwar once stood on the other side of this tactic. Now he is running it.

🎯 PH Callout

You promised Malaysia Madani — a civilised, reformist, accountable government. You promised that your administration would not criminalise dissent or use state machinery against political opponents. So what is this? A family exercises its legal right to challenge a police investigation and your response is to let the ‘Zionist plot’ narrative hang in the air like a threat. If you have evidence, present it in court. If you don’t, stop using parliamentary privilege to make accusations you can’t prove. The rakyat did not vote for another government that hides behind conspiracy theories when the questions get uncomfortable.

Sources: SCMP | Reuters | CNA

item.title: DAP Brings Forward Congress to July — Will Vote on Whether Leaders Should Quit Government, But Promises to Stay Anyway
item.description:
Said by: Anthony Loke, DAP Secretary-General / Transport Minister

Category: Politics

Date: 2026-03-09

📌 What Happened

DAP, the largest component party in Anwar’s Pakatan Harapan coalition by parliamentary seats, has brought forward its national congress from September to July 12, 2026. At the congress, more than 4,000 delegates will vote in an internal referendum on whether party leaders should resign from their government posts. DAP secretary-general and Transport Minister Anthony Loke told Malaysiakini on February 24 that “the government’s response has been inadequate and does not meet public expectations” regarding the MACC scandal. The move comes after DAP lost every seat it contested in November’s Sabah state polls and faces mounting grassroots anger over stalled reforms, the MACC crisis, and the perception that DAP is enabling a government that has abandoned its own platform.

🔥 Why It Matters

DAP’s congress is now the most important internal event on PH’s political calendar before the next general election. If an overwhelming majority of delegates vote for leaders to resign, it would destabilise Anwar’s government at the worst possible time — just as snap election preparations are underway. DAP supplies the single largest bloc of PH MPs, and losing its ministers would leave a vacuum in Cabinet. The referendum is also a proxy vote on whether DAP’s base still believes the unity government is worth supporting. For a party that built its brand on anti-corruption and institutional reform, the MACC scandal — and the government’s failure to act decisively — is existential.

💩 Why It’s Bullshit

Here’s the tell: DAP says its MPs will continue backing Anwar regardless of the referendum outcome. So the delegates get to feel empowered by voting for a dramatic gesture, while the party leadership has already pre-committed to changing nothing. It’s accountability theatre. Loke says the government’s response is inadequate, then stays in the government. DAP says reforms are a red line, then watches the PM tenure bill fail and the MACC chief remain in office. Bringing forward a congress to threaten to leave while promising to stay is not pressure politics. It’s a performance designed to placate the base while preserving power.

📜 Previous Cases

DAP has a pattern of loudly objecting and quietly complying. In 2023, the party protested UMNO’s influence in the unity government but stayed. In 2024, DAP MPs raised concerns about Najib’s house arrest deal but took no action. When Azam Baki’s shareholding scandal first surfaced in 2021-2022, DAP — then in opposition — demanded his suspension. Now in government, DAP waited years before its own secretary-general called the response “inadequate.” The Sabah wipeout in November 2025 should have been a wake-up call. Instead, DAP’s response is a congress five months away where the outcome has been pre-neutralised.

🎯 PH Callout

DAP, you told your voters you would be the conscience of the unity government. You said joining Anwar meant reform from the inside. Three years in, your own secretary-general admits the government’s response to corruption is inadequate. Your grassroots are furious. Your Sabah results were a humiliation. And your answer is a congress where delegates vote on a question you’ve already decided doesn’t matter? Either the referendum means something or it doesn’t. If your leaders stay regardless of the outcome, stop pretending this is democracy within the party. It’s damage control.

Sources: Straits Times | CNA | The Star

item.title: Deputy Economy Minister Says Growth Steady at 4-4.5% — Amid a Regional War and Global Volatility
item.description:
Said by: Mohd Shahar Abdullah, Deputy Economy Minister

Category: Economy

Date: 2026-03-09

📌 WHAT HAPPENED: On 9 March 2026, Deputy Economy Minister Mohd Shahar Abdullah projected 4–4.5% GDP growth for Malaysia at the Asean Banking and Finance Summit, citing domestic consumption, infrastructure investment, the digital economy, and Visit Malaysia 2026. He made this statement at the very summit whose chairman, Tan Sri Majid Khan, warned that geopolitical tensions are “repricing risk across our entire region.” The US-Iran conflict was escalating, oil prices were spiking due to Strait of Hormuz disruptions, and GPS jamming was affecting shipping across the Gulf.

🔥 WHY IT MATTERS: A deputy minister projecting steady growth at a summit convened to discuss regional instability suggests a dangerous disconnect between political messaging and economic reality. The growth drivers cited — tourism, digital economy, domestic consumption — are all vulnerable to the very geopolitical risks flagged at the same event. Oil price spikes may temporarily boost petroleum revenue, but they raise input costs across the economy and threaten the government’s already fragile fuel subsidy position. When your own summit chairman is sounding the alarm, doubling down on optimism reads less like confidence and more like denial.

💩 WHY IT’S BULLSHIT: Malaysia’s economy is heavily trade-dependent and exposed to every global shock the deputy minister chose to ignore.
